facts.text: Jericho was renamed in 1692 from Lusum after the biblical town near the Jordan River, reflecting its founders’ hope for a promised land.
The Old Jericho Friends Meeting House, built by early Quaker settlers, still stands today as a living reminder of the community’s 17th‑century roots.
Valentine Hicks’ home in Jericho served as a key Underground Railroad station, hiding runaway slaves behind a removable panel that still exists.
The Milleridge General Store, founded in 1672, is the oldest continuously operating business on Long Island and a testament to Jericho’s colonial heritage.
Jericho’s New York Community Bank Theatre, originally the Westbury Music Fair in 1956, has been a cultural hub for more than six decades.
Quaker families who settled in Jericho in the mid‑1600s fled persecution in England and New England, seeking peaceful farming life in the new colony.
